natural_sources: Human hair, duck feathers (historical commercial source)
natural_occurrence.description: A sulfur-containing amino acid that occurs naturally in proteins. Used in bread-making to break down gluten proteins, making dough more pliable and reducing mixing time.

manufacturing.description: Historically extracted from human hair (China) or duck/poultry feathers. Modern production primarily uses bacterial fermentation (E. coli) or chemical synthesis. Hair and feather sources have been phased out in most regions.

other_uses.household: Dietary supplements for skin and hair health

eu.legal_reference: Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 - not listed in Annex II

usa.notes: Approved as dough conditioner, up to 90 ppm in flour

australia_nz.notes: Permitted as processing aid for flour treatment

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (acceptable for use as amino acid)

regulatory_history.event: FDA approves L-cysteine as GRAS dough conditioner
EU bans L-cysteine as food additive
Media reports expose use of human hair from China as source
Major food companies pledge to use only synthetic or fermentation-derived L-cysteine

adi_context.source_note: No ADI established - L-cysteine is a natural amino acid. EU banned its use as food additive in 2004 for regulatory reasons, not safety concerns.

fun_facts: Historically made from human hair collected from barbershops in China
Also extracted from duck and chicken feathers
Banned in EU since 2004 - not for safety, but due to 'yuck factor'
The 'human hair in bread' scandal led to widespread consumer outcry
Now mostly produced by bacterial fermentation to avoid ethical concerns
Still used in US bagels and pizza dough to make it stretchy
Muslim and Jewish communities avoid it unless certified from acceptable sources
